title: Manage Risk Like The Best Hedge Funds With 42 Macro’s Dr. Mo Framework

Howdy investors. This is Darius Dell
here, founder and CEO of 42 Macro. And
today I'm going to talk to you about our
discretionary risk management overlay,
aka Dr. Mo, which is the systematic
system that we use to help our
professional investor clients and
sophisticated retail trader clients stay
on the right side of market risk, which
means maximizing upside capture in bull
markets and minimizing downside capture
in bare markets. So we'll start with our
42 macro risk management process. The
number one thing that we do for our
clients here at 42 macro is to help them
identify and position for the market
regime. We don't help them identify and
fight the market regime. We want them to
pos participate in what the markets are
trying to price in because that's
ultimately how you make the most money
in gold markets and minimize uh downside
capture uh in bare markets. And so how
do we do that? Uh we now cast the market
regime with our global macro risk matrix
system which we'll talk about shortly.
And then we filter those signals through
our KISS portfolio construction process
of which we have thousands of retail
investors around the world. Uh following
that simple three ETF solution to stay
on the right side of of market risk and
then our discretionary risk management
overlay which takes those same market
regime signals and generates proper
trade recommendations across 70
different factors across the equity,
fixed income, commodity, currency and
crypto markets. Uh and so uh when we say
what so for those who may be new what is
a market regime? If you go back and you
study some of the kind of hallowed halls
of institutional finance, what you'll
find is is that a lot of the best firms
do from an asset management standpoint
uh is use regime segmentation to orient
their factor long short bets. And so uh
generally speaking, there's about four
different regimes. Uh Goldilocks is a
risk regime in which investors uh it's a
risk-on regime with a disinflationary
bias in terms of the performance in
asset markets. Uh reflation is a risk-on
regime with an inflationary bias in
terms of the performance and dispersion
within and across asset markets.
Inflation is a riskoff regime uh with an
inflationary bias in terms of the
dispersion within and across asset
markets. And then uh deflation is a
riskoff regime uh with a disinflationary
bias in terms of the performance and
dispersion across asset markets. And so
generally speaking, in a riskoff regime,
you're going to want to be long
overweight risk assets and underweight
defensive assets. In a riskoff regime,
you're going to want to be doing the
opposite. Uh, generally speaking, in a
risk-on regime, you're going to be
overweight high beta as opposed to an
underweight low beta. And the opposite
is true in riskoff regimes. Uh, from a
sector standpoint, you're going to be
overweight cyclicals and underweight
defensives in riskon regime as opposed
to uh the opposite is true in riskoff
regimes. Uh, generally speaking, growth
tends to outperform value in riskon
regimes, whereas value tends to
outperform growth in inflation and
growth tends to outperform value in
deflation. Um, in terms of market cap
bias, uh, your factor long preferences,
you're going to be overweight, small,
and midcaps in a risk-on regime, and
underweight large caps. Uh, and then the
the opposite is true when you're in a
riskoff regime. Uh, from a regional
standpoint, uh, US tends to perform
international in Goldilocks, whereas
international tends to perform the US in
reflation and then the US tends to
perform both uh, in uh, in inflation and
deflation. Um, or sorry, US tends to
perform in both inflation and deflation.
uh EM tends to outperform DM uh when
you're in a risk-on regime and the
opposite is true in a riskoff regime uh
when you're uh in fixed income spread
products tend outperform treasuries in a
risk-on regime whereas the opposite is
true in a riskoff regime uh when you're
in uh thinking about uh allocating
across the treasury curve the short end
of the curve tends to outperform the
belly of the curve which tends to
outperform the long bond uh when you're
in a risk-on regime uh um the opposite
is true in a deflation regime and then
when you're in an inflation and so that
that is true across Goldilocks uh
reflation and inflation in terms of
short rates outperforming the belly
which tends to outperform the long bond
that is true in and again Goldilocks
reflation and inflation whereas in
deflation the performance is reversed
the long bond tends to outperform the
belly which tends to outperform short
rates. Um in with respect to credit if
you're in a riskon regime high yield
tends to outperform investment grade and
if you're in a riskoff regime investment
grade tends to outperform high yield. Uh
in commodities industrial commodities
tend out perform energy commodities
which tend outperform ad commodities in
a riskon regime. The opposite is true in
a riskoff regime. And then finally with
respect to currencies, gold tends to
outperform foreign currencies which tend
to outperform the US dollar. Uh in
riskon regimes um the the US dollar
tends to outperform gold which tends to
perform foreign currencies in inflation.
And then gold tends to outperform the US
dollar which tends to outperform foreign
currencies in deflation. And so what
investing from a what regime
segmentation does is it makes the
process of asset allocation and
portfolio construction much simpler,
much easier to think about and
ultimately uh uh it creates better
outcomes in your portfolio when you're
generally participating again in what
the market is trying to price in when
the market is trying to price it in. And
so uh let's talk about how we uh
determine what the actual market regime
is and how we send out timely and
actionable signals for our clients to
actually reorient their portfolios uh on
time so that they can continue
maximizing upside capture in bull
markets and minimizing downside capture
and bare markets. And so we use our
global macro risk matrix to now cast the
market regime which investors must
position for to avoid FOMO or FOMO. Fear
of missing out causes investors to buy
cycle tops and fear of more losses
causes investors to sell cycle lows. And
so how does this system work? Uh what we
looking at here in in this table are 42
of the most important markets uh market
prices market macro markets in the world
from the perspective of the
institutional investor community across
equities, volatility, uh commodities,
currencies and then fixed income across
credit and and sovereign uh debt. Uh and
so how does the system work? Uh we use a
couple of examples here. So we'll start
with the S&P 500 today. It is bearish
from the perspective of its own
individual volatility just a momentum
signal which is how we measure uh
volatility or how we measure momentum in
and across asset markets. And so that
bearish VA signal for the S&P 500 today
is attributing one point to each of the
two riskoff regimes go inflation uh and
deflation. Um for example the 10-year
Treasury yield broke down to bearish uh
today and historically the 10-year
Treasury yield has had a a negative
absolute performance in Goldilocks and
deflation. So each of those regimes is
currently uh getting a point. Uh and
then finally looking at something like
gold. Gold is bullish from the receptive
of its volatility just a momentum
signal. So gold has historically
performed well in every regime with the
exception of inflation. Uh so gold is
getting a uh the goldilocks is getting a
point. Inflation is getting a point and
deflation uh is getting a point. And so
how we determine the market regime we're
just looking at any given time which of
these four regimes has the highest sum
and share of confirming markets. And
right now it's inflation. Inflation
having 19 of the confirming markets. And
what we mean by confirming markets are
the ones that are actually generating a
bullish or bearish signal. The neutral
signals don't count because there's no
conviction in that trade for that
particular macro market exposure. Uh and
so you know what we say is we're in an
inflation market regime, then you're
going to want to orient your portfolio
uh uh to take advantage of the things
that tend to happen when you're in
inflation market regimes. at least
according to our back testing and the
generally accepted uh belief system
across institutional uh finance. Um you
know in terms of uh in terms of the
regime change statistics so you know
generally speaking there's about one and
a half to two uh uh what we call rorow
phase transitions per year. What a rorow
phase transition uh means is just that
you're going from a riskon regime to a
riskoff regime. As we showed here in
this slide, the factor long short
preferences when you're going from
riskon regime to risk on regime don't
really change that much. And the factor
long short preferences when you're going
from the two risk off regimes when
you're trans justing between the two
riskoff regimes don't really change that
much. It's when you go from risk on to
risk off or risk off to risk on where
you have to make significant changes in
your portfolio if you want to remain on
the right side of market risk. You don't
have to remain on the right side of
market risk, but I can guarantee you if
you're a professional investor managing
other people's money if you choose to
not remain on the right side of market
risk for a long enough period of time,
you won't have anybody else's money to
manage. And so, obviously, what the
system does is it makes our keeps our
buy side clients uh constantly oriented
towards what the markets are trying to
price in so they can get back to under,
you know, understanding uh what their
company fundamentals are, what their
credit fundamentals are for the for the
credits that they're investing in, etc.
Um so so as I mentioned there's about
you know let's call it two rorow phase
transitions per year which is where you
have this most significant amount of
change in your portfolio from a
portfolio construction and asset
allocation standpoint. So if you're in a
bull market what you tend to see uh is
you know you have one robo phase
transition to a deep enough correction
that that warrants risk managing as a
buy side professional and then you have
another role phase transition back to
the bull market. Uh if you're in a bare
market, what that ultimately looks like
is you have a big enough uh uh rally
counter trend rally in a bare market
that ultimately is big enough uh uh for
institutional investors to have to risk
manage uh um in order to uh essentially
stay on the stay in their seats if
they're by side professional. And then
there's another rural phase transition
back to the uh bare market. And so what
Dr. Mo does getting to Dr. Mo, what Dr.
MO does is takes that information from a
market regime now casting standpoint and
filters those signals across 70
different factors across uh US equities,
US equity factors, global equities,
fixed income sectors and macro exposures
which we interpret to be commodities,
currencies and crypto. Uh and it
generates proper trade recommendations
based on the combination of the market
regime as well as the combination of the
individual assets volatility adjusted
momentum signal. So for example, if an
ETF is bullish or bearish VMs, let's
just start with the bullish because it's
easier to explain this way. If an ETF is
bullish or if the factor is bullish and
it should be bullish in this regime,
then Dr. Mo will recommend a long max
position uh in that in that particular
exposure. Again, this is a market timing
and position sizing guide for factor
long short bets. If an asset is neutral,
but it should be bullish in that partic
in the particular market regime that
we're currently in, then Dr. MO
recommend a long half position in that
particular asset. Uh if an asset is
neutral and it should be bearish uh
based on how it's historically traded
based on our back testing here how it's
historically traded uh in in the
particular regime then Dr. Mo will
recommend a short half position and then
finally if an asset is bearish and it
should be bearish in the particular
regime then Dr. MO would recommend a a
short max position and then finally for
the assets where they are bullish but
they should be bearish in this regime
based on the historical back testing or
if the asset is bullish and it should be
or so bullish and it should be bearish
or if the asset is bearish and it should
be bullish uh then Dr. Mo will say hey
do nothing there's the market is is
sending conflicting signals this is your
your capital will be rewarded elsewhere
uh um for the time being and so it'll
recommend a a no position in those
particular assets. So, let's use a few
stylized examples so we can sort of
hammer home the the how easy and

adhering to these signals. U so let's

in this regime. And so as I mentioned,

here from Monday, March 24th, 2025. And
so until that market regime condition
changes, our clients are going to be

perspective and ultimately adhering to

are designed to take advantage of the
market risk provided by the inflation
market regime. Again, it's not about
hoping that we go into a Goldilocks or
reflation market regime or hoping that
we transition to a deflation market
regime. What Dr. Mo says, don't worry
about that. That will come in the
future. For now, take advantage of the
market risk that the the the returns
that the markets are trying to give you
uh if you're paying attention. You don't
have to pay attention. You don't have to
stay on the right side of market risk.
But as I mentioned, if you stay on the
wrong side of market risk for a long
enough time as a professional investor,
you're not going to have anybody else's
money to manage. So ultimately what we
found throughout our travels across the
global buy side uh and our clients you
know we we currently advised well over
$25 trillion and in and in and client
AUM what we found is that you know
essentially so much of the job for most
buysiders has become to orienting their
portfolios to take advantage of what the
market's pricing in not enough investors
on the buy side anymore have enough time
horizon or duration to just invest in
the companies and the credits that they
ultimately believe in the most. And so,
you know, so part of the job what we've
built this system is to help acknowledge
that and ultimately take advantage of of
that. And so, and it works really, it's
incredibly effective. And so, as you
know, we back test everything very
rigorously with institutional grade back
tests here uh with respect to um with
respect to all of our signals, Dr. Mo
included. Uh and so, if you wanted to
isolate some of the key macro kind of
factors, the macro markets that matter
to most investors, what we find is that
our Dr. Most signals are incredibly
effective at again helping investors
stay on the right side of market risk
which it means maximizing upside capture
in bull markets and minimizing downside
capture in bare markets. And so in terms
of how to interpret these charts, you
know what this uh the the bars in these
charts just identify the cumulative
performance by proper trade
recommendation. So this is the total
amount of performance you would have uh
achieved just being long these assets
since January of 1998. uh in terms of on
a on a in terms of summing the the daily
log price changes for each of these
assets and then what the dotted lines uh
indicate is the percentage of that
cumulative performance that has occurred
during the the as as a function of each
of these proper trade recommendations.
And so what we ultimately see is that
when Dr. Mo is recommending a long
position for the S&P 500. Historically,
the 110% of the return, the cumulative
performance that we've seen in the S&P
500 since January of 1998 has come when
Dr. Mo recommended a long position in
the S&P 100. And so, for example, what
does that mean? So, if the S&P 500 was
up 100% uh since January of 1998, it's
up somewhere closer to, you know, 300
400%. But if it was up just
stylistically, if it if if if it was up
100%, you would have made 110%. If there
was a dollar to be made, uh, uh, you
would have actually made $110 just by
being long the S&P 500 when Dr. Mo tells
you to be long and not being
participating in the asset when Dr. Mo
tells you not to participate. And the
reason that's incredibly important for
institutional investors is because when
you're long an asset, when you're
supposed to be long an asset based on
its volatility, digital momentum signal
characteristics and the market regime
signal characteristics, you tend to have
lower volatility, lower draw downs when
you're in those moments. And so
ultimately your riskadjusted returns
increase when you're participating in
the asset when you're supposed to be
participating and not participating in
the asset when you're not supposed to be
participating when the volatility of the
asset is higher when the draw downs are
steeper and ultimately your risk
adjusted returns are lower uh in those
assets. Uh for global equities 140% of
the cumulative performance since January
of 1998 has come uh when you uh when Dr.
Mo's recommending a long position. So
for instance, if you if there was a
dollar to be made just being naked long
buy and hold global equities since
January of 1998, you would have made
a$140 if you were only long them uh when
Dr. Mo says be long. Uh that upside
capture ratio is 105% for the long bond.
It's 114% for international bonds. It's
103% for investment grade credit. It's
116% for high yield credit. It's 1,700%
essentially for for the dollar, which by
the way, the dollar, you know, doesn't
have much cumulative performance because
it's a a stationary object over over a
long enough time horizon. And so, uh,
obviously those results are a bit
skewed, but again, it it continues to
signal that if you're long when you're
supposed to be long, particularly for an
asset that has a a high degree of of of
of medium diversion like a currency
would, then you actually improving your
risk adjusted returns by even that much
more. Um, the upside capture ratio for
gold is 94%, the upside capture ratio
for commodities is 240%, it's 158% for
crude oil. And then finally for the
crypto market, the upside capture ratio
uh for Bitcoin is 108% and 150% for
Ethereum. So what that again what that
means is that if you're long Bitcoin
when Dr. Mo tells you to be long
Bitcoin, you would have made a$18 as
opposed to the dollar you could have
made if you were just naked long buy and
hold Bitcoin, which is what the bear the
bull porn purveyors would have you do.
And the issue with the the the the bull
porn out there in the crypto space is
crypto has much more significant draw
downs than most asset classes. I want to
say Bitcoin specifically has declined
about 75 to 90% once every four years
since the inception of the asset class.
And so obviously that may not matter to
you if you're 25 or 30 something years
old and you don't have that much money
you've accumulated over the course of
your career uh in your portfolio. But if
you're in your 50s or 60s or heaven
forbid you're retired, you can afford to
suffer a 75 to 90% draw down in a in a
in a in a core strategic holding. And so
ultimately, you need systems like Dr. Mo
to keep you on the right side of market
risk, which means be long when you're
supposed to be long and don't be long or
be short when you're supposed to be
short. Uh and so just be aware of that.
And again, that upside capture ratio is
even better for Ethereum since the
inception of asset class at 150%. So you
would have made a buck 50 being long
Ethereum as opposed to the dollar you
could have made since Ethereum was first
launched. So uh that's our Dr. Mo
system. Again, it's an incredibly
effective system. It sends out clear,
actionable and timely proper trade
recommendations uh based on the market
regime uh that we're currently now
casting our ourselves in and ultimately

from the perspective of its volatility
